name: Avenc des Cocarroi
equipment: Vertical progression gear and a 20 m rope. No installation equipment needed as the rope can be fixed to rocks.
description: A modest 11 m deep pothole in the karstic landscape of Escorca, notable for its entrance being completely concealed by ivy covering its walls and floor, giving it an air of mystery. The vertical walls show no formations but have water channels carved into them. The cave may have historically served as a snow pit, evidenced by carefully placed stones covering a drain. Wild goats occasionally fall in, and the trip can be combined with an ascent of nearby Puig de Femenias.
full_description:
The place where this sinkhole is located can be defined as a place where the karstic landscapes, shaped mainly by the dissolution of rocks by water that through filtration form a drainage system where numerous caves and sinkholes develop.

This pothole has the peculiarity that its mouth is covered by ivy that is adhered to its walls in such a way that it is impossible to see the bottom despite its wide mouth. With an approximate depth of a little more than 11 meters, the walls are completely vertical without any concretions, although several channels are carved by the water sliding down its walls.

It is worth noting that the ivy is not only found in the upper part of the pothole but has covered the entire surface of both the walls and the bottom, giving it an air of a certain halo of mystery, although a very pleasant one. The bottom is horizontal with a drain on its eastern side covered with some well-placed stones, enough for me to dare to say that we were not the first to have accessed the pothole, as it may have been used as a snow house (a pit for storing snow) in other times, a trade that has now disappeared.

This type of potholes also inadvertently serve as true traps for the feral goats that roam the Serra de Tramuntana, since, without going any further, the remains of two of them were found at the bottom, and in the nearby pothole another one had also fallen in.

The day can be complemented by ascending Puig de Femenias, which is found on the way back before descending through the pass that allows us to reach the lower level, with the visible summit that we will climb 'rough-style,' that is, without any type of markers signaling the path. We also have a small pothole 169 m away, already on the way back, at coordinates 490460 4412646 DATUM EUROPEAN 1950, which we can also access through its thick mantle of ivy.
